risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A statistician performed the allocation of the patients using a computer program." Comment: This describes a computer-based randomization process conducted by a statistician, which is sufficient evidence of a proper random sequence generation method. The additional note about sealed opaque envelopes pertains more to allocation concealment than sequence generation.

Quote: "A statistician performed the allocation of the patients using a computer program. Aiming to avoid selection bias, a third person numbered and sealed the opaque envelopes." Comment: This explicitly describes sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes (SNOSE): the envelopes were numbered, sealed, and opaque, which is an adequate concealment mechanism preventing the person enrolling participants from foreseeing assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The patients were allocated in two groups: 1) Exercise Group (ExG) (n = 50) 2) Orientation Group (OG) (n = 50)." and "The patients from the Orientation group received an explanation about the manual after the initial evaluation... Over a period of eight weeks, the patients received two phone calls..." and "The patients from the ExG group took part in group sessions lasting from 30 to 40 minutes, with a weekly frequency of twice a week... The patients from the ExG also received the orientation manual given to the OG group." Comment: The interventions were obviously different (supervised exercise sessions versus orientation/manual with phone calls), so participants and treating personnel were not plausibly blinded to group assignment. The main outcomes included pain (NRS) and quality of life (SF-36), which are subjective and could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation; therefore performance bias is high.

Quote: "The pre and post-intervention assessments were carried out by a physiotherapist blinded to the patient's group." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by a clinician/independent assessor (physiotherapist), and blinding of the assessor is explicitly stated. Although the study included subjective outcomes (pain NRS and SF-36 quality of life) as well as a more performance-based TUG test, the reported blinding of the outcome assessor addresses the most bias-vulnerable outcomes, so detection bias is judged low risk.

Quote: "One hundred patients were selected... 1) Exercise Group (ExG) (n = 50) 2) Orientation Group (OG) (n = 50)." and "Eighty-one patients (n = 43 in ExG and n = 38 in OG) finished the study... In OG, one patient fractured his ankle and 11 did not return for the final assessment even after being contacted by phone." and "For the intention to treat (ITT) analysis... we used mixed model variance analysis (ANOVA) with repeated measurements... There was no data input in the ITT..." Comment: Post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcomes at the 8-week post-intervention assessment was 19/100 overall (19%), with imbalance between arms: 7/50 (14%) lost in ExG versus 12/50 (24%) in OG, a 10 percentage-point differential. Reasons also include adverse/inflammatory knee problems in ExG and many unexplained non-returns in OG. Although a mixed-model ITT analysis was used, the reported differential attrition reaches the threshold for concern and could bias the randomized comparison.
